verses.flow: And the LORD called to Moses and spoke to him from the tent of meeting, saying,
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Leviticus begins exactly where Exodus ends -- the glory has filled the tabernacle (Exod 40:34-35), and now the LORD speaks FROM the tent. The Hebrew "vayiqra" (and He called) is the book's title. The small aleph in the traditional Hebrew text at the end of vayiqra is noted by the rabbis -- Moses' humility before the call of God.

verses.flow: "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them: When any man among you brings an offering to the LORD, you shall bring your offering from the livestock -- from the herd or from the flock.
verses.note: HEBREW: "qorban" (offering) comes from the root "qarav" (to draw near). Every sacrifice is fundamentally about drawing near to God. The worshipper does not merely give something -- he comes close. This is the heart of all sacrificial theology.

verses.flow: "If his offering is a burnt offering from the herd, he shall offer a male without blemish; he shall bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting, so that he may be accepted before the LORD.
verses.note: CHRIST: "Without blemish" (tamim) -- the same word used of Noah (Gen 6:9) and required of the Passover lamb (Exod 12:5). Christ is the ultimate tamim: "a lamb without blemish or spot" (1 Pet 1:19). The burnt offering (olah, "that which ascends") represents total consecration -- the entire animal goes up in smoke. Christ's total self-offering to God (Heb 9:14; Eph 5:2).

verses.flow: "And he shall lay his hand on the head of the burnt offering, and it shall be accepted for him to make atonement on his behalf.
verses.note: CHRIST: The laying on of hands (semikah) is the act of identification and transfer. The worshipper leans his weight on the animal's head, symbolically transferring his identity to the substitute. This is the foundation of imputation theology -- the animal dies in the worshipper's place. Paul: "He made Him who knew no sin to be sin on our behalf" (2 Cor 5:21). The Hebrew "kaphar" (to make atonement) literally means "to cover." The blood covers sin from God's sight.

verses.flow: "And what remains of the grain offering shall belong to Aaron and his sons -- a most holy portion from the offerings by fire to the LORD.
"No grain offering that you bring to the LORD shall be made with leaven, for you shall not burn any leaven or any honey as an offering by fire to the LORD.
verses.note: LEAVEN AND HONEY: Both cause fermentation -- a process of decay and corruption. Leaven consistently symbolizes sin and corruption in Scripture (Matt 16:6; 1 Cor 5:6-8). No corruption may ascend to God on the altar.

verses.flow: "As an offering of firstfruits you may bring them to the LORD, but they shall not go up on the altar for a pleasing aroma.

"And every offering of your grain offering you shall season with salt, and you shall not let the salt of the covenant of your God be lacking from your grain offering. With all your offerings you shall offer salt.
verses.note: SALT: "The salt of the covenant" -- salt preserves and purifies, the opposite of leaven. Every offering must have salt because every offering represents an enduring covenant relationship. Mark 9:49-50 alludes to this: "Everyone will be salted with fire." The covenant is permanent, incorruptible.

verses.flow: "And if his offering is a sacrifice of peace offerings -- if he brings it from the herd, whether male or female, he shall offer it without blemish before the LORD.
verses.note: HEBREW: "shelamim" (peace offerings) comes from "shalom" -- wholeness, completeness, well-being. Unlike the burnt offering (entirely consumed) or sin offering (priestly portions), the peace offering is a shared meal: God receives the fat, the priest receives portions, and the worshipper eats the rest with family and friends. This is the fellowship offering -- communion at the table of God. It prefigures the Lord's Supper.

verses.flow: "And the priest shall burn them on the altar as food, an offering by fire for a pleasing aroma. All the fat belongs to the LORD.
verses.note: FAT BELONGS TO GOD: The fat represents the best, the richest part. In every sacrifice, regardless of type, the fat goes to God. The principle: the best belongs to the LORD. The worshipper may eat the meat, but the choicest portion ascends to God.

verses.flow: "It shall be a perpetual statute throughout your generations in all your dwelling places: you shall not eat any fat or any blood."
verses.note: PERPETUAL STATUTE: No fat, no blood -- ever. Fat belongs to God (the best is His); blood belongs to God (life itself is His). These two prohibitions frame the entire sacrificial system and extend to all Israel's meals. The theological statement: life and the best of life are God's domain, not man's to claim.

verses.flow: "Speak to the sons of Israel, saying: When a person sins unintentionally in any of the things which the LORD has commanded not to be done, and does any one of them --
verses.note: HEBREW: "bishgagah" (unintentionally) -- the sin offering covers inadvertent sin, not deliberate rebellion (for which see Num 15:30-31, "with a high hand"). This is crucial: even unintentional sin requires blood atonement. Sin is not merely a moral choice -- it is a condition that contaminates regardless of intent. The sacrificial system teaches that we are more sinful than we know.

verses.flow: "if it is the anointed priest who sins, thus bringing guilt on the people, then let him offer for the sin which he has committed a bull of the herd without blemish to the LORD as a sin offering.
verses.note: GRADUATED SACRIFICE: The anointed priest brings a bull (most costly), the congregation brings a bull, a leader brings a male goat, a commoner brings a female goat or lamb. Greater privilege = greater responsibility = costlier atonement. "To whom much is given, much is required" (Luke 12:48). The priest's sin "brings guilt on the people" -- leadership sin has corporate consequences.

verses.flow: "Command Aaron and his sons, saying: This is the law of the burnt offering. The burnt offering shall remain on the hearth on the altar all night until the morning, and the fire of the altar shall be kept burning on it.
verses.note: LAW OF THE BURNT OFFERING: The fire never goes out. The burnt offering burns all night, all day, perpetually. This is the "tamid" -- the continual offering (Exod 29:38-42). Morning and evening, every day, the smoke ascends. Christ's intercession is perpetual: "He always lives to make intercession" (Heb 7:25).

"And if any of the flesh of the sacrifice of his peace offerings is eaten on the third day, it shall not be accepted, and it shall not be credited to the one who offers it. It shall be an abomination, and the person who eats of it shall bear his iniquity.
verses.note: THIRD DAY: Meat not eaten by the third day becomes "piggul" (an abomination). This strange rule embeds a time-limit on the sacrificial meal. Some scholars see a distant echo: Christ was raised on the third day, before His body saw corruption (Ps 16:10; Acts 2:27). verses.note: BLOOD PROHIBITION: This is one of the most emphatic commands in Leviticus. Blood = life (17:11), and life belongs exclusively to God. verses.note: SUMMARY: This verse catalogs the entire sacrificial system in one sentence: burnt, grain, sin, guilt, ordination, peace. Six types covering every dimension of the God-human relationship -- total devotion, daily provision, fellowship, atonement for condition, atonement for acts, consecration for service. Christ fulfills all six.

verses.note: FIRE FROM GOD: Divine fire consumes the offering -- God accepts. This is the same fire that fell on Elijah's altar (1 Kgs 18:38) and the same fire of Pentecost (Acts 2:3). verses.flow: And Nadab and Abihu, the sons of Aaron, each took his censer and put fire in it and placed incense on it and offered unauthorized fire before the LORD, which He had not commanded them.
verses.note: UNAUTHORIZED FIRE: Hebrew "esh zarah" -- strange fire, alien fire. The exact nature of their sin is debated, but the text is clear: "which He had not commanded them." The fire on the altar came from God (9:24); Nadab and Abihu brought fire from another source. They innovated in worship. They substituted human initiative for divine instruction. This is the foundational sin of unauthorized worship -- approaching God on human terms rather than His.

verses.flow: Then Moses said to Aaron, "This is what the LORD has spoken, saying: 'Among those who are near to Me I will be treated as holy, and before all the people I will be glorified.'" And Aaron was silent.
verses.note: AARON WAS SILENT: Hebrew "vayiddom Aharon" -- one of the most devastating sentences in Scripture. Aaron says nothing. His sons are dead, killed by the God they were consecrated to serve, and he is silent. This is not stoic composure -- it is the silence of a man who knows God is right. He cannot protest because he understands: holiness is not negotiable. Cf. Ps 39:9: "I was mute; I did not open my mouth, because it was You who did it."

verses.flow: And Aaron said to Moses, "Behold, today they offered their sin offering and their burnt offering before the LORD, and yet such things as these have happened to me. If I had eaten the sin offering today, would it have been good in the eyes of the LORD?"
verses.note: AARON'S ANSWER: Aaron finally speaks, and his answer reveals pastoral wisdom: "Such things have happened to me -- would God accept my eating the sin offering today?" He has just lost two sons to divine judgment. His grief, though unexpressed publicly, is real, and he judges (correctly) that eating the holy meal while in such a state would be inappropriate. Moses accepts this. Even within the strict system, there is room for human brokenness before God.

verses.note: THE PIG: The pig is the emblematic unclean animal -- it has the external sign (split hoof) but lacks the internal reality (does not chew the cud). The rabbis saw this as hypocrisy: the pig "sticks out its hooves as if to say, See, I am clean" while hiding its true nature. Jesus uses similar language about the Pharisees: clean outside, unclean within (Matt 23:25-28).

verses.note: BE HOLY FOR I AM HOLY: The theological foundation of the dietary laws and all Levitical legislation. Holiness is not an attribute God recommends -- it is what He IS, and His people must reflect His nature. 1 Pet 1:15-16 quotes this verse directly: "You shall be holy, for I am holy." The dietary laws are training wheels for holiness -- daily practice in saying no to the common in order to embrace the consecrated.

verses.flow: "And the person with the leprous disease -- his garments shall be torn and the hair of his head shall hang loose, and he shall cover his upper lip and cry out, 'Unclean, unclean!'
verses.note: CRY "UNCLEAN": The leper must publicly announce his condition -- torn clothes, loose hair, covered lip, and the cry "Unclean, unclean!" This is devastating social exile AND a living sermon. Every person who encounters the leper hears the word that describes all humanity before God. When Christ touches the leper (Mark 1:41), He reverses the entire system: instead of the clean becoming unclean by contact, the unclean becomes clean.

verses.flow: "All the days that the disease is on him he shall be unclean. He is unclean. He shall dwell alone; his dwelling shall be outside the camp.
verses.note: OUTSIDE THE CAMP: The leper dwells alone, outside the camp -- excluded from community, from worship, from normal life. This is the fullest picture of what sin does: it isolates, excludes, and exiles. Christ was crucified "outside the camp" (Heb 13:12), taking the leper's place. He bore our exile so we could be brought near.

verses.note: THEOLOGY: The mercy seat (kapporet, from "kaphar" -- to cover/atone) is the gold lid of the ark where God's visible glory (shekinah) appeared between the cherubim. It is the intersection of heaven and earth, the place where God meets man. The high priest could approach only once per year, only with blood, only with incense to shield himself from the lethal holiness of God. CHRIST: Hebrews 4:14-16 declares that through Jesus, believers may now "draw near with confidence to the throne of grace." The veil has been torn (Matt 27:51).

verses.flow: And Aaron shall cast lots over the two goats: one lot for the LORD and one lot for Azazel.
verses.note: AZAZEL: The Hebrew "la'azazel" is one of the most debated terms in the OT. Three main interpretations: (1) a place name meaning "rugged cliff" (the goat is sent to a desolate place); (2) an abstract noun meaning "complete removal" (the goat carries sins away entirely); (3) the name of a wilderness demon or fallen angel. In 1 Enoch 8-10, Azazel is a fallen watcher who taught humanity forbidden knowledge and upon whom the sins of the world are placed (1 En 10:8). The Dead Sea Scrolls (4Q180) connect Azazel to the leader of the fallen angels. Whether the biblical text identifies a supernatural being or not, the function is clear: one goat dies (substitutionary atonement), the other carries sins away permanently. CHRIST: Both goats are fulfilled in Christ -- He dies as the sacrifice (Heb 9:12) and He carries sins away never to return (Heb 9:26; John 1:29; Isa 53:4-6).

verses.flow: And Aaron shall bring the goat on which the lot for the LORD fell and offer it as a sin offering.
But the goat on which the lot for Azazel fell shall be presented alive before the LORD, to make atonement over it, to send it away to Azazel into the wilderness.
verses.note: THEOLOGY: The scapegoat is not sacrificed but sent away alive "to Azazel." The laying on of hands (v.21) transfers Israel's sins onto the goat, which then bears them into the wilderness -- into the uninhabited, chaotic realm outside the ordered world of God's people. Sin is not merely forgiven but removed, carried away to a place of no return. CHRIST: "As far as the east is from the west, so far has He removed our transgressions from us" (Ps 103:12). Christ is both the slain goat (His death) and the scapegoat (sin's permanent removal).

The high priest lays both hands on the live goat's head and confesses "all the iniquities... all their transgressions... all their sins." verses.flow: For the life of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it to you on the altar to make atonement for your souls, for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life.
verses.note: THEOLOGY: This is one of the most important theological statements in the entire Torah. "The life (nephesh) of the flesh is in the blood" -- blood is not merely a biological substance but the carrier of life itself. God has "given" the blood specifically for the altar, specifically for atonement. The blood makes atonement "by the life" (b'nephesh) -- it is life-for-life exchange. CHRIST: This verse is the foundation of all substitutionary atonement theology. "Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins" (Heb 9:22). Christ's blood is His life poured out -- "the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered Himself without blemish to God" (Heb 9:14).

verses.flow: And when you reap the harvest of your land, you shall not fully reap the corner of your field, nor shall you gather the gleaning of your harvest.
verses.note: THEOLOGY: The gleaning laws are one of the most beautiful ethical provisions in the Torah. The farmer must deliberately leave the edges of his field unharvested and not go back for dropped grain. This is not charity but justice -- the poor have a right to glean. It preserves the dignity of the poor (they work for their food) while requiring generosity from the landowner. RUTH: The entire book of Ruth unfolds in the gleaning fields of Bethlehem (Ruth 2), and Boaz's generosity beyond the law leads to the lineage of David and ultimately Christ.

verses.note: HEBREW: "mo'adim" (appointed times) -- from "ya'ad," to meet by appointment. These are not merely festivals but divine appointments, scheduled meetings between God and His people. The same word is used in Gen 1:14 for the heavenly bodies that mark "appointed times." The entire created order is calibrated to God's calendar. CHRIST: Every mo'ed is prophetically fulfilled in Christ, making the festival calendar a prophetic timeline of redemption.

In the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month at twilight, is the Passover of the LORD.
verses.note: PASSOVER: The Passover lamb slain on the 14th of Nisan is the foundational redemptive event of Israel's history. The lamb's blood on the doorposts (Exod 12) causes the destroyer to "pass over." CHRIST: "Christ our Passover has been sacrificed" (1 Cor 5:7). Jesus was crucified on Passover (John 19:14), at the very hour the Passover lambs were being slaughtered in the temple. The timing is not coincidental but divinely orchestrated.

But in the seventh year there shall be a Sabbath of solemn rest for the land, a Sabbath to the LORD. You shall not sow your field, and you shall not prune your vineyard.
verses.note: SABBATH FOR THE LAND: Just as humans rest on the seventh day, the land rests in the seventh year. The earth itself participates in the Sabbath rhythm of creation. This is not merely agricultural wisdom but theological conviction: the land belongs to God (v.23) and deserves the rest He ordains. Israel's failure to keep the land Sabbaths led to the 70-year exile (2 Chr 36:21) -- the land forcibly took the rest Israel refused to give it.

verses.note: REPENTANCE: The restoration promise is conditional on confession and humility. "If they confess... if then their uncircumcised heart is humbled..." verses.flow: And yet for all that, when they are in the land of their enemies, I will not reject them, nor will I abhor them to destroy them utterly, to break My covenant with them, for I am the LORD their God.
verses.note: THEOLOGY: Even in the extremity of judgment, God will not "reject... abhor... destroy utterly... or break My covenant." The covenant is unconditional in its ultimate preservation even though it is conditional in its immediate blessings. God's faithfulness outlasts Israel's unfaithfulness. This is the bedrock of biblical hope. CHRIST: "If we are faithless, He remains faithful, for He cannot deny Himself" (2 Tim 2:13).

verses.flow: However, anything that a man devotes to the LORD from all that he has, whether man or animal or the field of his possession, shall not be sold or redeemed. Every devoted thing is most holy to the LORD.
verses.note: HEBREW: "cherem" (devoted thing) -- the most extreme category of dedication to God. Something placed under cherem is irrevocably given to God and cannot be bought back. In warfare, this meant total destruction (Josh 6:17-18). In votive contexts, it means permanent, unredeemable consecration. The severity of the cherem underscores the absolute nature of certain commitments to God.

words.g: the ruddy i.e. a human being

And all the tithe of the land, whether of the seed of the land or of the fruit of the tree, is the LORD's. It is holy to the LORD.
verses.note: TITHE: "All the tithe of the land... is the LORD's. It is holy to the LORD." The tithe (ma'aser, literally "a tenth") acknowledges that everything belongs to God and the tenth is His designated portion. The tithe preceded the Mosaic law (Abraham tithed to Melchizedek, Gen 14:20) and continued beyond it. CHRIST: Jesus affirmed tithing while warning against neglecting "the weightier matters of the law: justice and mercy and faithfulness" (Matt 23:23).

verses.flow: These are the commandments that the LORD commanded Moses for the sons of Israel on Mount Sinai.
verses.note: CONCLUSION: "These are the commandments that the LORD commanded Moses for the sons of Israel on Mount Sinai." This closing formula bookends the entire Sinai legislation that began in Exodus 19. The location -- Sinai -- and the mediator -- Moses -- authenticate the divine origin of everything between. Leviticus ends as it began: with the voice of God speaking from the place where heaven touched earth.
